question_en: Which of the following is NOT a fundamental principle of British life?

options.en: Tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs

explanation_en: The fundamental principles of British life include: tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs, the rule of law, democracy, individual liberty and participation in community life.

question_en: Which TWO of the following are environmental charities?
explanation_en: The National Trust and Friends of the Earth are environmental charities.

question_en: Which flag has a diagonal red cross on a white ground?

options.en: The cross of St George, patron saint of England
The cross of St Patrick, patron saint of Ireland
The cross of St David, patron saint of Wales
The cross of St Andrew, patron saint of Scotland

explanation_en: The cross of St Patrick, patron saint of Ireland, is a diagonal red cross on a white ground.

question_en: Where is the city of Swansea located?
explanation_en: Swansea is located in Wales .

question_en: When is Christmas Eve celebrated?
explanation_en: Christmas Eve is celebrated on the 24th of December.

question_en: Which British sportsman won five consecutive gold medals at the Olympic Games in the rowing category?
explanation_en: Sir Steve Redgrave won gold medals in rowing in five consecutive Olympic Games and is one of Britain’s greatest Olympians.

question_en: What is the name of the centrepiece to the Remembrance Day service located in Whitehall, London?
explanation_en: The Cenotaph in Whitehall is the site of the annual Remembrance Day service attended by the King, politicians and foreign ambassadors.

question_en: Who was the inventor of the World Wide Web?
explanation_en: The inventor of the World Wide Web, Sir Tim Berners-Lee (1955-), is British. Information was successfully transferred via the web for the first time on 25 December 1990.

question_en: What does the UK offer to its residents or citizens (choose FIVE answers)?

options.en: A right to a fair trial
A right to join in the election of a government

explanation_en: The UK offers to its residents or citizens: freedom of belief and religion, freedom of speech, freedom from unfair discrimination, a right to a fair trial and a right to join in the election of a government.

question_en: When did the Wars of the Roses start?
explanation_en: In 1455, a civil war was begun to decide who should be king of England. It was fought between the supporters of two families: the House of Lancaster and the House of York. This war was called the Wars of the Roses, because the symbol of Lancaster was a red rose and the symbol of York was a white rose.

question_en: How many members does the Scottish Parliament have?
explanation_en: There are 129 members of the Scottish Parliament (MSPs), elected by a form of proportional representation.

question_en: What does the term ‘coalition’ refer to?

options.en: If no political party wins a majority, a new election is held to vote for one of the top two parties only
The candidate who gets the most votes is elected
If no political party wins a majority, two parties may join and govern together
If an MP dies or resigns, there will be a fresh election

explanation_en: The government is usually formed by the party that wins the majority of constituencies. If no party wins a majority, two parties may join together to form a coalition.

question_en: The public can listen to debates in the Palace of Westminster from public galleries in both the House of Commons and the House of Lords.
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: Pool and darts are traditional pub games.
explanation_en: True.

question_en: When did the UK formally leave the European Union?
explanation_en: The UK formally left the European Union on 31 January 2020 .

question_en: What is the minimum age requirement in the UK to drink wine or beer with a meal provided you are with someone over 18?
explanation_en: When they are 16, people can drink wine or beer with a meal in a hotel or restaurant (including eating areas in pubs) as long as they are with someone over 18.

question_en: Who is the spiritual leader of the Church of England?
explanation_en: The spiritual leader of  the Church of England is the Archbishop of Canterbury .

question_en: Civil servants cannot stand for public office:
explanation_en: Most citizens of the UK, Ireland or the Commonwealth aged 18 or over can stand for public office. There are some exceptions, including: members of the armed forces, civil servants, people found guilty of certain criminal offences.

question_en: What does the UK offer to its permanent residents or citizens?

options.en: A right to join in the election of a government
Free access to all national museums

explanation_en: A right to join in the election of a government is the correct answer.

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a fundamental principle of British life?

options.en: Tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs

explanation_en: The fundamental principles of British life include: tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs, the rule of law, democracy, individual liberty and participation in community life.

question_en: What do you need to apply to become a permanent resident or citizen of the UK (choose TWO options)?

options.en: To be able to speak and read English
A good understanding of life in the UK

explanation_en: The UK offers to its residents or citizens: freedom of belief and religion, freedom of speech, freedom from unfair discrimination, a right to a fair trial and a right to join in the election of a government.

question_en: What does the UK offer to its residents or citizens (choose FIVE answers)?

options.en: A right to a fair trial
A right to join in the election of a government

explanation_en: To apply to become a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you will need to speak and read English and have a good understanding of life in the UK .

question_en: Which of the following is a fundamental principle of life in the UK?

options.en: Intolerance of those with different beliefs

explanation_en: The fundamental principles of British life include: democracy, the rule of law, individual liberty , tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs, and participation in community life.

question_en: Which TWO of the following responsibilities will you have as a permanent resident or citizen of the UK?

options.en: To respect and obey the law
To go to church on Sundays
To look after the area in which you live and the environment

explanation_en: If you wish to be a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you should respect and obey the law, respect the rights of others, including their right to their own opinions, treat others with fairness, look after yourself and your family and look after the area in which you live and the environment.

question_en: Participation in community life is a fundamental principle of British life.
explanation_en: The fundamental principles of British life include: participation in community life .

question_en: To look after yourself and your family is one of the responsibilities that you will have as a permanent resident or citizen of the UK.
explanation_en: This statement is TRUE .

question_en: In which of the following languages is the life in the UK test NOT available?
explanation_en: The Life in the UK test is usually taken is English, although special arrangements can be made if you wish to take it in Welsh or Scottish Gaelic.

question_en: Which of the following statements is true?

options.en: To apply to become a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you will need to have a university degree
To apply to become a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you will need to have a good understanding of life in the UK
To apply to become a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you will need to have British relatives
To apply to become a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you will need to have knowledge of the British laws

explanation_en: To apply to become a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you will need to have a good understanding of life in the UK and be able to speak and read English.

question_en: Which TWO of the following are fundamental principles of British life?

options.en: Tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs

explanation_en: Tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs and the rule of law are fundamental principles of British life.

question_en: Which TWO of the following islands are known as ‘Crown dependencies’?
explanation_en: There are also several islands which are closely linked with the UK but are not part of it: the Channel Islands and the Isle of Man. These have their own governments and are called ‘Crown dependencies’.

question_en: What does the UK offer in return to its residents or citizens?

options.en: A right to a fair trial

explanation_en: A right to a fair trial is the correct answer.

question_en: Which of the following territories does not belong to Great Britain?
explanation_en: ‘Great Britain’ refers only to England, Scotland and Wales, not to Northern Ireland.

question_en: Which of the following are British overseas territories?
explanation_en: There are several British overseas territories in other parts of the world, such as St Helena and the Falkland Islands . They are also linked to the UK but are not part of it.

question_en: Where is the Parliament of the UK located?
explanation_en: The UK is governed by the parliament sitting in Westminster .

question_en: St Helena is a British overseas territory.
explanation_en: British overseas territories in other parts of the world include St Helena and the Falkland Islands . They are also linked to the UK but are not part of it.

question_en: The Isle of Man is a Crown dependency.
explanation_en: There are several islands which are closely linked with the UK but are not part of it: the Channel Islands and the Isle of Man. These have their own governments and are called ‘Crown dependencies’.

question_en: British values and principles are based on history and traditions and are protected by law, customs and expectations.
explanation_en: The Channel Islands and the Isle of Man are closely linked to the UK but are not part of it.

question_en: Which of the following lines does NOT form part of the citizenship pledge?

options.en: I will give my loyalty to the United Kingdom
I will uphold its democratic values
I will observe its laws faithfully

explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: As part of the citizenship ceremony, new citizens pledge their loyalty to the United Kingdom and to respect its rights and freedoms.
explanation_en: Long live our noble King! is the second sentence of the National Anthem of the UK.

question_en: Which of the following are ’Crown dependencies’?

options.en: The Isle of Man and the Channel Islands
The Isle of Wight and Northern Ireland

explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: Which TWO islands are closely linked to the UK but are not part of it?
explanation_en: The Isle of Man and the Channel Islands are ’Crown dependencies’.

question_en: Which of the following sentences is true?

options.en: The UK is governed by the parliament sitting in Edinburgh.
Scotland and Wales have parliaments or assemblies of their own, but not Northern Ireland.
Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland also have parliaments or assemblies of their own, with devolved powers in defined areas.
The UK is governed by the parliament sitting in Glasgow.

explanation_en: Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland also have parliaments or assemblies of their own, with devolved powers in defined areas.

question_en: In which English county is the monument of Stonehenge located?
explanation_en: The ancestors of the first farmers probably came from south-east Europe. These people built houses, tombs and monuments on the land. One of these monuments, Stonehenge, still stands in what is now the English county of Wiltshire .

question_en: What is the best-known work from Robert Burns?
explanation_en: Burns’ best-known work is probably the song Auld Lang Syne , which is sung by people in the UK and other countries when they are celebrating the New Year (or Hogmanay as it is called in Scotland).

question_en: Which of the following crosses does NOT form part of the Union Flag?

options.en: The cross of St George, patron saint of England
The cross of St David, patron saint of Wales
The cross of St Patrick, patron saint of Ireland
The cross of St Andrew, patron saint of Scotland

explanation_en: The cross of St David, patron saint of Wales does not form part of the Union Flag because, when the first Union Flag was created in 1606 from the flags of Scotland and England, the Principality of Wales was already untied with England.

question_en: What day is Halloween celebrated?
explanation_en: Halloween is celebrated on the 31st of October .

question_en: Who was the first British distance runner to win a gold medal in the Olympic games in the 10,000 metres?
explanation_en: Mo Farah (1983-) is a British distance runner, born in Somalia. He won gold medals in the 2012 Olympics for the 5,000 and 10,000 metres and is the first Briton to win the Olympic gold medal in the 10,000 metres.

question_en: Which of the following statements is TRUE?

options.en: For some Scottish people, Hogmanay is a bigger holiday than Christmas
For some Scottish people, Hogmanay is a bigger holiday than New Year’s Eve
For some Welsh people, Hogmanay is a bigger holiday than Christmas
For some Irish people, Hogmanay is a bigger holiday than New Year’s Eve

explanation_en: In Scotland, the 31st December is called Hogmanay and 2 January is also a public holiday. For some Scottish people, Hogmanay is a bigger holiday than Christmas.

question_en: Light-hearted plays with music and comedy, which are enjoyed by family audiences and based on fairy stories are known as:
explanation_en: Many theatres produce a pantomime at Christmas time. They are based on fairy stories and are light-plays with music and comedy, enjoyed by family audiences.

question_en: What British actor was best known for his roles in various Shakespeare plays?
explanation_en: The Laurence Olivier Awards take place annually at different venues in London. There are a variety of categories, including best director, best actor and best actress. The awards are named after the British actor Sir Laurence Olivier , late Lord Olivier, who was best known for his roles in various Shakespeare plays.

question_en: Who built the Tower of London after becoming king in 1066?
explanation_en: The Tower of London was first built by William the Conqueror after he became king in 1066.

question_en: Who succeeded David Cameron as Prime Minister after the referendum on the UK’s membership of the European Union?
explanation_en: David Cameron was succeeded as Prime Minister after the Brexit referendum by Theresa May on 13 July 2016.

question_en: Who composed a suite of pieces themed around the planets and the solar system called ‘The Planets’?
explanation_en: Important British composers include Gustav Holst , whose work includes The Planets, a suite of pieces themed around the planets and the solar system.

question_en: What was the population of the UK in 1998?
explanation_en: The population of the UK in 1998 was 57 million people.

question_en: Who is the chief officer of the House of Commons?
explanation_en: Debates in the House of Commons are chaired by the Speaker . This person is the chief officer of the House of Commons.

question_en: What characterises the British constitution?

options.en: It was written by the Prime Minister
It was written by the King
It is not written down in any single document, and therefore it is described as ‘unwritten
It was written by Henry VIII

explanation_en: The British constitution is not written down in any single document, and therefore it is described as ‘unwritten’.

question_en: How old was Queen Victoria when she became Queen in 1837?
explanation_en: In 1837, Queen Victoria became queen of the UK at the age of 18 .

question_en: When did the First World War start?
explanation_en: On 28 June 1914 , Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria was assassinated. This set off a chain of events leading to the First World War (1914-18).

question_en: Who forms the judiciary?
explanation_en: Judges (who are together called ‘the judiciary’) are responsible for interpreting the law and ensuring that trials are conducted fairly.

question_en: Which of the following is a musical venue located in London?
explanation_en: There are many large venues that host music events throughout the year, such as: The O2 in Greenwich, south-east London.

question_en: Who directed the movie ‘The Killing Fields’ in 1984?
explanation_en: The Killing Fields (1984) was directed by Roland Joffé .

question_en: Which TWO of the following castles are located in Scotland?
explanation_en: Inveraray Castle and Crathes Castle are located in Scotland.

question_en: Which battle signified the end of the Wars of the Roses?
explanation_en: The Wars of the Roses ended with the Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485.

question_en: Which court deals with minor criminal offences in Scotland?

options.en: The Justice of the Peace Court

explanation_en: In Scotland, minor criminal offences go to a Justice of the Peace Court .

question_en: If an MP dies or resigns, there will be a fresh election, which is known as:
explanation_en: If an MP dies or resigns, there will be a fresh election, called a by-election , in his or her constituency.

question_en: A person who has not paid enough National Insurance contributions will not be able to receive certain contributory benefits including (choose TWO options):
explanation_en: Anyone who does not pay enough National Insurance Contributions will not be able to receive certain contributory benefits such as Jobseeker’s Allowance or a full state retirement pension .

question_en: Who succeeded Theresa May as Prime Minister on the 24th July 2019?
explanation_en: Theresa May was succeeded by Boris Johnson on 24 July 2019.

question_en: What name is given to the period of rapid development of industry in Britain in the 18th and 19th centuries?
explanation_en: The Industrial Revolution was the rapid development of industry in Britain in the 18th and 19th centuries.

question_en: During the slave trade, slaves were taken from West Africa to America and the Caribbean to work on tobacco and sugar plantations.
explanation_en: This statement is TRUE .

question_en: Who was responsible for the construction of the Great Western Railway?
explanation_en: Isambard Kingdom Brunel was responsible for constructing the Great Western Railway, which was the first major railway built in Britain.

question_en: In which of the following areas has the Senedd the power to make laws (choose four answers)?
explanation_en: The Senedd has the power to make laws in 21 areas, including: education and training, health and social services, economic development and housing.

question_en: How many members comprise the UN Security Council?
explanation_en: There are 15 members on the UN Security Council, which recommends action when there are international crises and threats to peace.

question_en: Which TWO of the following forts form part of Hadrian’s Wall?
explanation_en: Parts of Hadrian’s Wall include the forts of Housesteads and Vindolanda .

question_en: Members of the Welsh government can speak in either English or Welsh, but all of the Senedd’s publications have to be in English:
explanation_en: Members of the Welsh government can speak in either English or Welsh, and all publications of the Senedd’s publications are in both languages.

question_en: Why did Elizabeth I keep her cousin Mary prisoner for 20 years?

options.en: She did not agree with her political views
She suspected Mary of wanting to take over the English throne
She was a spy of the Spanish Armada

explanation_en: Elizabeth suspected Mary of wanting to take over the English throne, and kept her prisoner for 20 years.

question_en: What was the name of the first cloned mammal?
explanation_en: In 1996, two British scientists, Sir Ian Wilmot and Keith Campbell, led a team which was the first to succeed in cloning a mammal, Dolly the sheep.

question_en: Who is responsible for the creation of the European Convention on Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms?
explanation_en: The Council of Europe has no power to make laws but draws up conventions and charters, the most well-known of which is the European Convention on Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms.

question_en: Who became Prime Minister of the UK in May 1940?
explanation_en: Churchill was the son of a politician and, before becoming a Conservative MP in 1900, was a soldier and journalist. In May 1940 he became Prime Minister.

question_en: Members of the House of Lords may stand for election to the House of Commons and are eligible for all public offices:
explanation_en: Members of the House of Lords may not stand for election to the House of Commons but are eligible for all other public offices.

question_en: What can you find at the ‘Eden Project’?

options.en: Giant greenhouses, house plants from all over the world
Animals from all over the world
An open market with local food

explanation_en: The Eden Project is located in Cornwall, in the south west of England. Its biomes, which are like giant greenhouses, house plants from all over the world . The Eden Project is also a charity which runs environmental and social projects internationally.

question_en: What TWO names are given to the people who give tours at the Tower of London?
explanation_en: Tours to the Tower of London are given by the Yeoman Warders , also known as Beefeaters , who tell visitors about the building’s history.

question_en: Which of the following is a traditional Scottish food?
explanation_en: Haggis – a sheep’s stomach stuffed with offal, suet, onions and oatmeal – is a traditional Scottish food.

question_en: How often do the Laurence Olivier Awards take place?
explanation_en: The Laurence Olivier Awards take place annually at different venues in London.

question_en: When is Valentine’s Day?
explanation_en: Valentine’s Day is celebrated on the 14th of February .

question_en: When is St Patrick’s Day, patron to of Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: St Patrick’s Day, patron to of Northern Ireland is on the 17th of March .

question_en: What does the term ‘PDSA’ stand for?
explanation_en: ‘PDSA’ stands for People’s Dispensary for Sick Animals .

question_en: Which of the following TWO islands are ‘Crown dependencies’?
explanation_en: There are several islands which are closely linked with the UK but are not part of it: the Channel Islands and the Isle of Man . These have their own governments and are called ‘Crown dependencies’.

question_en: When did English settlers begin to colonise the eastern coast of America?
explanation_en: In Elizabeth I’s time, English settlers first began to colonise the eastern coast of America.

question_en: When did the Black Death come to England?
explanation_en: In 1348 , a disease, probably a form of plague, came to Britain. This was known as the Black Death.

question_en: What is the name of the annual event that gives awards in a range of musical categories, such as best British group and best British solo artist?
explanation_en: The Brit Awards is an annual event that gives awards in a range of categories, such as best British group and best British solo artist.

question_en: What name is given to the elected members of the Welsh government?
explanation_en: In Wales the elected members are known as SMs or Senedd Members .

question_en: How often do the ministers that form the Cabinet meet to make important decisions about government policy?
explanation_en: The cabinet is a committee which usually meets weekly and makes important decisions about government policy.

question_en: How many Welsh-language channels are there in Wales?
explanation_en: There is one Welsh-language channel in Wales.

question_en: What is the nickname for the great bell of the clock at the Houses of Parliament in London?
explanation_en: Big Ben is the nickname for the great bell of the clock at the House of Parliament in London.

question_en: How many countries are members of the United Nations?
explanation_en: The UK is part of the United Nations (UN), an international organization with more than 190 countries as members.

question_en: Which of the following statements is true?

options.en: The police force is a public service that helps and protects everyone
The police force is a private service that helps and protects everyone
The police force is a private service in charge of rescuing people from dangerous situations
The police force is a public service that works to avoid road accidents

explanation_en: The police force is a public service that helps and protects everyone, no matter what their background or where they live.

question_en: What is the system by which the income tax is automatically taken from the employee’s income by their employer and paid directly to HM Revenue & Customs (HMRC)?
explanation_en: For most people, the right amount of income tax is automatically taken from their income from employment by their employer and paid directly to HM Revenue & Customs (HMRC), the government department that collects taxes. This system is called “Pay As You Earn” (PAYE) .

question_en: How many British casualties were recorded during the First World War?
explanation_en: During the First World War millions of people were killed or wounded, with more than 2 million British casualties .

question_en: Why did Henry VIII decide to divorce Catherine of Aragon?

options.en: She was unpopular in the country
She was too old to give him another child
She was accused of taking lovers to the Palace

explanation_en: When Catherine was too old to give him another child, Henry VIII decided to divorce her, hoping that another wife would give him a son to be his heir.

question_en: How many local authorities are there in London?
explanation_en: London has 33 local authorities , with the Greater London Authority and the Mayor of London coordinating policies across the capital.

question_en: When did British combat troops leave Iraq?
explanation_en: British combat troops left Iraq in 2009 .

question_en: Where is the Parliament of the UK?
explanation_en: The UK is governed by the parliament sitting in Westminster .

question_en: Which of the following is a criminal offence?

options.en: Selling tobacco to someone under the age of 18

explanation_en: It is illegal and a criminal offence to sell tobacco products (for example, cigarettes, cigars, roll-up tobacco) to anyone under the age of 18.

question_en: ﻿Who used a system of land ownership known as feudalism?
explanation_en: The Normans used a system of land ownership known as feudalism.

question_en: The line ‘all the world’s a stage’ belongs to which of the following plays or poems written by William Shakespeare?
explanation_en: All the world’s a stage’ are lines from William Shakespeare’s play As You Like It .

question_en: During the times of Elizabeth I, English settlers began to colonise:
explanation_en: In Elizabeth I’s time, English settlers first began to colonise the eastern coast of America .

question_en: In which city is the Welsh government building, also known as Senedd, located?
explanation_en: In Wales the elected members, known as SMs, meet at the Senedd in Cardiff Bay .

question_en: What architectural style became popular in the 19th century?
explanation_en: In the 19th century, the medieval ’gothic’ style became popular again. As cities expanded, many great public buildings were built in this style. The Houses of Parliament and St Pancras Station were built at this time, as were the town halls in cities such as Manchester and Sheffield.

question_en: Which of the following gardens is NOT located in England?
explanation_en: There are famous gardens to visit throughout the UK, including Kew Gardens, Sissinghurst and Hidcote in England, Crathes Castle and Inveraray Castle in Scotland, Bodnant Garden in Wales, and Mount Stewart in Northern Ireland.

question_en: Which TWO families started a civil war to decide who should be king of England in 1455?
explanation_en: In 1455, a civil war was begun to decide who should be king of England. It was fought between the supporters of two families: the House of Lancaster and the House of York .

question_en: Who was in charge of the British fleet at the Battle of Trafalgar?
explanation_en: Admiral Nelson was in charge of the British fleet at Trafalgar and was killed in the battle.

question_en: Which court deals with serious criminal offences in Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland, serious offences are tried in front of a judge and a jury in a Crown Court . In Scotland, serious cases are heard in a Sheriff Court with either a sheriff or a sheriff with a jury.

question_en: Which political party does the Speaker represent?

options.en: They do not represent any political party

explanation_en: The Speaker is neutral and does not represent a political party, even though he or she is an MP, represents a constituency and deals with the constituents’ problems like any other MP.

question_en: When was the Scottish Parliament formed?
explanation_en: The Scottish Parliament was formed in 1999 .

question_en: What characterised the Bronze Age?

options.en: People were farmers who lived in large villages
People lived in roundhouses and buried their dead in tombs

explanation_en: During the Bronze Age people lived in roundhouses and buried their dead in tombs called round barrows.

question_en: What name is given to the elected members of the Scottish Parliament?
explanation_en: Elected members of the Scottish Parliament are known as Members of the Scottish Parliament (MSPs).

question_en: Which game do people usually play at Halloween?
explanation_en: During Halloween young people will often dress up in frightening costumes to play ‘trick or treat’ .

question_en: The public can listen to debates in the Palace of Westminster from public galleries located in:

options.en: Both, the House of Commons and House of Lords
All of the above are false

explanation_en: The public can listen to debates in the Palace of Westminster from public galleries in both the House of Commons and the House of Lords .

question_en: What is the most well-known gallery in Wales?
explanation_en: In the UK, some of the most well-known galleries are The National Gallery, Tate Britain and Tate Modern in London, the National Museum in Cardiff, and the National Gallery of Scotland in Edinburgh.

question_en: The Man Booker Prize for Fiction is awarded annually for the best fiction novel written by an author from:

options.en: The European Union, Zambia or Zimbabwe
The Commonwealth, the USA or Australia
The European Union, Canada or Australia

explanation_en: The Man Booker Prize for Fiction is awarded annually for the best fiction novel written by an author from the Commonwealth, Ireland or Zimbabwe . It has been awarded since 1968.

question_en: Which of the following statements regarding Gaelic language is TRUE?

options.en: In Wales, Gaelic is spoken in some parts of the coast
In Scotland, Gaelic is spoken everywhere
In Scotland, Gaelic is spoken in some parts of the Highlands and Islands
In Wales, Gaelic is the main language

explanation_en: In Scotland, Gaelic is spoken in some parts of the Highlands and Islands, and in Northern Ireland some people speak Irish Gaelic.

question_en: Participation in community life is a fundamental principle of British life.
explanation_en: The fundamental principles of British life include: participation in community life.

question_en: How many British citizens left the country between 1853 and 1913?
explanation_en: Between 1853 and 1913, as many as 13 million British citizens left the country.

question_en: Where was the main Olympic site for the 2012 Games?
explanation_en: The main Olympic site for the 2012 Games was in Stratford , East London.

question_en: Who forms the shadow cabinet?

options.en: Senior opposition MPs appointed by the leader of the opposition
Junior opposition MPs appointed by the leader of the governing party
Junior opposition MPs appointed by the leader of the opposition

explanation_en: The leader of the opposition also appoints senior opposition MPs to be ‘shadow ministers’. They form the shadow cabinet and their role is to challenge the government and put forward alternative policies.

question_en: Who directed the movie ‘Brief Encounter’ in 1945?
explanation_en: Brief Encounter (1945) was directed by David Lean .

question_en: Who was the leader of the English Republic?
explanation_en: Oliver Cromwell was the leader of the English republic.

question_en: According to the 2011 Census, what percentage of the population identified themselves as Hindu?
explanation_en: In the 2011 Census, 59% of people identified themselves as Christian. Much smaller proportions identified themselves as Muslim (4.8%), Hindu (1.5%) , Sikh (0.8%), Jewish or Buddhist (both less than 0.5%).

question_en: People in the UK have to pay tax on any earnings from (choose THREE options)?

options.en: Income from property, savings and dividends

explanation_en: People in the UK have to pay tax on their income, which includes: wages from paid employment, profits from self-employment, taxable benefits, pensions and income from property, savings and dividends.

question_en: How many devolved administrations are there in the UK?
explanation_en: There are three devolved administrations in the UK, these include: the Welsh government, the Scottish Parliament and the Northern Ireland Assembly.

question_en: Who is responsible for interpreting the law and ensuring that trials are conducted fairly?

options.en: The Prime Minister and the MPs

explanation_en: Judges (who are together called ‘the judiciary’ ) are responsible for interpreting the law and ensuring that trials are conducted fairly.

question_en: How often do people who are 70 years old or over need to renew their driving licence?
explanation_en: Drivers can use their driving licence until they are 70 years old. After that, the licence is valid for three years at a time.

question_en: Where was Margaret Thatcher born?
explanation_en: Margaret Thatcher was the daughter of a grocer from Grantham in Lincolnshire .

question_en: Where are the Welsh government and the Senedd based?
explanation_en: The Welsh government and Senedd are based in Cardiff , the capital city of Wales.

question_en: Jenson Button is a famous:
explanation_en: Jenson Button is a famous Formula 1 driver .

question_en: Who introduced the Statute of Rhuddlan by which Wales was annexed to the Crown of England in 1284?
explanation_en: In 1284 King Edward I of England introduced the Statute of Rhuddlan, which annexed Wales to the Crown of England.

question_en: When was the Brexit referendum held?
explanation_en: The Brexit referendum was held on 23 June 2016 .

question_en: In the UK, alcohol cannot be sold to anyone under the age of:
explanation_en: It is a criminal offence to sell alcohol to anyone who is under 18 or to buy alcohol for people who are under the age of 18. (There is one exception: people aged 16 or over can drink alcohol with a meal in a hotel or restaurant.

question_en: Who was known as the Iron Duke?
explanation_en: In 1815, the French Wars ended with the defeat of the Emperor Napoleon by the Duke of Wellington at the Battle of Waterloo. Wellington was known as the Iron Duke and later became Prime Minister.

question_en: Who appoints the members of the Cabinet?
explanation_en: The Prime Minister (PM) is the leader of the political party in power. He or she appoints the members of the cabinet and has control over many important public appointments.

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a British invention?
explanation_en: The World Wide Web, the Turing machine and the hovercraft are British inventions. The mobile phone is not.

question_en: When did Argentina invade the Falklands Islands?
explanation_en: In 1982 , Argentina invaded the Falkland Islands, a British overseas territory in the South Atlantic.

question_en: Which of the following statements is true?

options.en: County Courts deal with a wide range of civil disputes including family matters, breaches of contract and divorce.
County Courts deal with a wide range of criminal disputes including family matters, breaches of contract and divorce.

explanation_en: County Courts deal with a wide range of civil disputes including family matters, breaches of contract and divorce.

question_en: When is Boxing Day?
explanation_en: Boxing Day is on the 26th of December , the day after Christmas Day and is a public holiday.

question_en: According to 2011 Census, what percentage of the population identified themselves as Sikh?
explanation_en: In the 2011 Census, 59% of people identified themselves as Christian. Much smaller proportions identified themselves as Muslim (4.8%), Hindu (1.5%), Sikh (0.8%) , Jewish or Buddhist (both less than 0.5%).

question_en: Which court usually deals with serious criminal cases in Scotland?
explanation_en: In Scotland, serious criminal cases are heard in a Sheriff Court with either a sheriff or a sheriff with a jury. The most serious cases in Scotland, such as murder, are heard at a High Court with a judge and jury.

question_en: Which of the following are British overseas territories?
explanation_en: There are several British overseas territories in other parts of the world, such as St Helena and the Falkland Islands . They are also linked to the UK but are not part of it.

question_en: When did Alexander Fleming win the Nobel Prize in medicine for the discovery of the penicillin?
explanation_en: Fleming won the Nobel Prize in Medicine in 1945 for the discovery of the penicillin.

question_en: What is Sir Jackie Stewart famous for?

options.en: He captained the English football team that won the World Cup in 1966
He captained the English cricket team
He won the Formula 1 world championship three times
He won gold medals in rowing in five consecutive Olympic Games

explanation_en: Sir Jackie Stewart is a Scottish former racing driver who won the Formula 1 world championship three times.

question_en: The decade of the 1960s was a period of significant social change, when there was growth in British fashion, cinema and popular music:
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: When did Britain and France develop the world’s only supersonic commercial airliner known as ‘The Concorde’?
explanation_en: The 1960s was a time of technological progress, when Britain and France developed the world’s only supersonic commercial airliner, Concorde.

question_en: When was the United Nations set up?

options.en: Before the War of the Roses

explanation_en: The UN was set up after the Second World War and aims to prevent war and promote international peace and security.

question_en: Where can you find copies of the ‘Hansard’?

options.en: In large libraries and at www.parliament.uk

explanation_en: Proceedings in Parliament are broadcast on television and published in official reports called Hansard. Written reports can be found in large libraries and at www.parliament.uk .

question_en: Which court normally deals with cases of children aged 10 to 17 in England, Wales and Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland, if an accused person is aged 10 to 17, the case is normally heard in a Youth Court in front of up to three specially trained magistrates or a District Judge. The most serious cases will go the Crown Court.

question_en: How old do you need to be to be able to go into betting shops or gambling clubs?
explanation_en: You have to be 18 to go into betting shops or gambling clubs.

question_en: Which flag has a diagonal white cross on a blue ground?

options.en: The cross of St Andrew, patron saint of Scotland
The cross of St Patrick, patron saint of Ireland
The cross of St George, patron saint of England
The cross of St David, patron saint of Wales

explanation_en: The cross of St Andrew, patron saint of Scotland, is a diagonal white cross on a blue ground.

question_en: How long do Muslims fast for during Ramadan?
explanation_en: Eid a-Fitr celebrates the end of Ramadan, when Muslims have fasted for a month .

question_en: Which British actor has recently won an Oscar?
explanation_en: Recent British actors to have won Oscars include Colin Firth , Sir Antony Hopkins, Dame Judi Dench, Kate Winslet and Tilda Swinton.

question_en: Which was the first major railway built in Britain?
explanation_en: The Great Western Railway was the first major railway built in Britain.

question_en: In the 15th century, Henry Tudor, the leader of the House of Lancaster, became:
explanation_en: In 1485, Henry Tudor, the leader of the House of Lancaster, became King Henry VII after defeating King Richard III of the House of York at the Battle of Bosworth Field.

question_en: How many senior MPs are appointed by the Prime Minister?
explanation_en: The Prime Minister appoints about 20 senior MPs to become ministers in charge of departments.

question_en: Who won an Olympic gold medal in the pentathlon in 1972?
explanation_en: Mary Peters was a talented athlete who won an Olympic gold medal in the pentathlon in 1972.

question_en: What is the capital city of Wales?
explanation_en: The capital city of Wales is Cardiff .

question_en: When did the Roman army leave Britain?
explanation_en: The Roman army left Britain in AD 410 to defend other parts of the Roman Empire and never returned.

question_en: Which of the following statements is true?

options.en: You will receive a fine up to £2000 if you watch TV but do not have a TV licence.
You will receive a fine up to £1,000 if you watch TV but do not have a TV licence.
You will receive a fine up to £10,000 if you watch TV but do not have a TV licence.
You will receive a fine up to £5,000 if you watch TV but do not have a TV licence.

explanation_en: You will receive a fine up to £1,000 if you watch TV but do not have a TV licence.

question_en: Emmeline Pankhurst set up the women’s Franchise League in 1889, which fought to get the vote in local elections for married women.
explanation_en: This statement is true . Emmeline Pankhurst was born in Manchester in 1858. She set up the women’s Franchise League in 1889, which fought to get the vote in local elections for married women.

question_en: The king is the head of state in the UK:
explanation_en: King Charles III is the head of state of the UK.

question_en: How long did the First World War last for?
explanation_en: The First World War lasted four years (1914 – 1918).

question_en: Who was Henry VII?

options.en: The leader of the House of York
The leader of the House of Lancaster
The leader of the House of Buckingham
The leader of the House of Leeds

explanation_en: Henry VII was the leader of the House of Lancaster .

question_en: When were the Houses of Parliament built?
explanation_en: The Houses of Parliament and St Pancras Station were built in the 19th century , as were the town halls in cities such as Manchester and Sheffield.

question_en: How many volunteers work for the National Trust?
explanation_en: There are now more than 61,000 volunteers helping to keep the National Trust running.

question_en: What was the population of the UK in 1851?
explanation_en: The population of the UK in 1851 was 20 million people .

question_en: The line ‘a rose by any other name’ belongs to which of the following plays or poems written by William Shakespeare?
explanation_en: ‘A rose by any other name’ is a line from William Shakespeare’s play Romeo and Juliet .

question_en: Which religious community celebrates Vaisakhi?
explanation_en: Vaisakhi (also spelled Baisakhi) is a Sikh festival which celebrates the founding of the Sikh community known as the Khalsa.

question_en: Which book written by JRR Tolkien was voted the country’s best-loved novel in 2003?
explanation_en: In 2003, The Lord of the Rings by JRR Tolkien was voted the country’s best-loved novel.

question_en: The Senedd has 129 Senedd members.
explanation_en: The Senned has 60 Senedd members and the Scottish Parliament has 129 members.

question_en: Which British scientist made important discoveries working with steam power?
explanation_en: James Watt ’s work on steam power, helped the progress of the Industrial Revolution.

question_en: How can you visit the Northern Ireland Assembly (choose TWO options)?

options.en: Arranging a tour through the visitor services

explanation_en: There are two ways to arrange a visit to the Northern Ireland Assembly at Stormont. You can either contact the Education Service (details are on the Northern Ireland Assembly website at http://www.niassembly.gov.uk) or contact an MLA .

question_en: When was the first television broadcast made?
explanation_en: In 1932 Scotsman John Logie Baird made the first television broadcast between London and Glasgow.

question_en: For what sort of claims is the small claims procedure in Northern Ireland used for?
explanation_en: The small claims procedure is used for claims of less than £3,000 in Northern Ireland.

question_en: How many Russian and Polish Jews came to Britain to escape prosecution between 1870 and 1914?
explanation_en: Between 1870 and 1914, around 120,000 Russian and Polish Jews came to Britain to escape persecution.

question_en: Which Anglo-Saxon poem is about a hero’s battle against monsters and has been translated into modern English?
explanation_en: The Anglo-Saxon poem Beowulf tells of its hero’s battles against monsters and is still translated into modern English.

question_en: Which of the following movies was directed by Alfred Hitchcock in 1935?
explanation_en: The 39 Steps (1935) was directed by Alfred Hitchcock.

question_en: When was the National Health System (NHS) established?
explanation_en: In 1948 , Aneurin (Nye) Bevan, the Minister for Health, led the establishment of the National Health Service (NHS), which guaranteed a minimum standard of health care for all, free at the point of use.

question_en: The King has reigned since her mother’s death in 2022.
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: What is the note with the highest value in the UK?
explanation_en: The note with the highest value in the UK is the £50 note.

question_en: Who was Prime Minister of Britain from 1721 to 1742?
explanation_en: Sir Robert Walpole was Prime Minister from 1721 to 1742.

question_en: What is celebrated during Diwali?

options.en: Jesus Christ rising from the dead
The victory of good over evil and the gaining of knowledge
The founding of the Sikh community known as the Khalsa
The beginning of the winter season

explanation_en: Diwalli celebrates the victory of good over evil and the gaining of knowledge.

question_en: Who chairs the debates in the House of Commons?
explanation_en: Debates in the House of Commons are chaired by the Speaker .

question_en: The Battle of Hastings is commemorated in a great piece of embroidery, which is known as:
explanation_en: The Battle of Hastings is commemorated in a great piece of embroidery, known as the Bayeux tapestry , which can still be seen in France today.

question_en: A woman who is violent towards her partner cannot be prosecuted in the UK:
explanation_en: False . Anyone who is violent towards their partner – whether they are a man or a woman, married or living together – can be prosecuted.

question_en: According to the 2011 census, what percentage of the population identified themselves as Christian?
explanation_en: According to the 2011 Census, 59% of people identified themselves as Christians.

question_en: Many MPs, SMs and MSPs hold regular local events where constituents can go in person to talk about issues that are of concern to them. These are known as:
explanation_en: Many MPs, SMs and MSPs  hold regular local ‘surgeries’ , where constituents can go in person to talk about issues that are of concern to them. These surgeries are often advertised in the local newspaper.

question_en: Which golf ‘Major’ tournament is hosted by a different golf course every year?
explanation_en: The open championship is the only ‘Major’ tournament held outside United States. It is hosted by a different golf course every year.

question_en: Which act introduced the Forced Marriage Protection Orders for England, Wales and Northern Ireland in 2008?
explanation_en: Forced Marriage Protection Orders were introduced in 2008 for England, Wales and Northern Ireland under the Forced Marriage (Civil Protection) Act 2007 . Similar Protection Orders were introduced in Scotland in November 2011.

question_en: Which of the following countries did NOT join the European Economic Community when it was first formed in 1957?
explanation_en: West Germany, France, Belgium, Italy, Luxembourg and the Netherlands formed the European Economic Community (EEC) in 1957. At first the UK did not wish to join the EEC but it eventually did so in 1973.

question_en: How many members does the Northern Ireland assembly have?
explanation_en: The Northern Ireland Assembly has 90 elected members , known as MLAs (members of the Legislative Assembly). They are elected with a form of proportional representation.

question_en: Which of the following civil laws include disputes between landlords and tenants over issues such as repairs and eviction?
explanation_en: Housing law includes disputes between landlords and tenants over issues suck as repairs and eviction.

question_en: How can you visit the Scottish Parliament?

options.en: Arranging a tour through the visitor services

explanation_en: To visit the Scottish Parliament, you can get information, book tickets or arrange tours through visitor services. You can also write to them at the Scottish Parliament, Edinburgh, EH99 1SP, telephone 0131 348 5200 or email sp.booking@scottish.parliament.

question_en: Which of the following gardens is located in Wales?
explanation_en: There are famous gardens to visit throughout the UK, including Kew Gardens, Sissinghurst and Hidcote in England, Crathes Castle and Inveraray Castle in Scotland, Bodnant Garden in Wales, and Mount Stewart in Northern Ireland.

question_en: Who was reining in Britain when the British Empire became the largest empire the world has ever seen, with an estimated population of more than 400 million people?
explanation_en: During the Victorian period, the British Empire grew to cover all of India, Australia and large parts of Africa. It became the largest empire the world has ever seen, with an estimated population of more than 400 million people.

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a duty of the police?
explanation_en: The job of the police in the UK is to protect life and property, prevent disturbances (also known as keeping the peace) and prevent and detect crime.

question_en: The UK is not part of which of the following international institutions?
explanation_en: The UK is a member of the Council of Europe, the United Nations and the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO). The UK is no longer a member of the European Union.

question_en: Where is the National Gallery of Scotland located?
explanation_en: The National Gallery of Scotland is in Edinburgh .

question_en: Where are the ancestors of the first farmers to arrive in Britain believed to come from?
explanation_en: The first farmers arrived in Britain 6,000 years ago. The ancestors of these first farmers probably came from south-east Europe .

question_en: What religion did Elizabeth I follow?
explanation_en: Queen Elizabeth I was a Protestant .

question_en: Who directed the movie ‘Chariots of Fire’ in 1981?
explanation_en: Chariots of Fire (1981) was directed by Hugh Hudson .

question_en: What do new citizens have to swear or affirm as part of the citizenship ceremony?

options.en: They will remain in the UK
They will go to church at least once a week

explanation_en: New citizens swear or affirm loyalty to the King as part of the citizenship ceremony.

question_en: In which TWO cases may a person who has been summoned to do jury service be exempted from doing it:

options.en: If they have a criminal conviction
If they provide a good reason to be excused, such as ill health
If they have to go to work

explanation_en: Everyone who is summoned to do jury service must do it unless they are not eligible (for example, because they have a criminal conviction) or they provide a good reason to be excused, such as ill health.

question_en: What is the role of the shadow cabinet?

options.en: To keep order during political debates to make sure the rules are followed
To challenge the government and put forward alternative policies
To point out what they see as the government’s failures and weaknesses

explanation_en: Incorrect The role of the shadow cabinet is to challenge the government and put forward alternative policies.

question_en: Which hill fort from the Iron Age can be seen in the county of Dorset?
explanation_en: A very impressive hill fort from the Iron Age can still be seen today at Maiden Castle , in the English county of Dorset.

question_en: On which of the following issues the Northern Ireland Assembly CANNOT make decisions?
explanation_en: The Northern Ireland Assembly can make decisions on issues such as education, agriculture, the environment, health and social services.

question_en: Who was the first person to sail single-handed around the world in 1966/67?
explanation_en: A British sailor, Sir Francis Chichester , was the first person to sail singlehanded around the world, in 1966/67.

question_en: In 1837, Queen Victoria became queen of the UK at the age of 20.
explanation_en: In 1837, Queen Victoria became queen of the UK at the age of 18 .

question_en: Who led the group of Catholics who tried to kill the Protestant king with a bomb in the Houses of the Parliament in 1605?
explanation_en: In 1605, a group of Catholics led by Guy Fawkes failed in their plan to kill the Protestant king with a bomb in the Houses of Parliament. This is the origin of the Bonfire Night celebration on the 5th November, when people in Great Britain set off fireworks at home or in special displays.

question_en: What was the biggest source of employment in Britain before the 18th century?
explanation_en: Before the 18th century, agriculture was the biggest source of employment in Britain.

question_en: In which city is the Scottish Parliament building, also known as Holyrood, located?
explanation_en: In Scotland the elected members, called MSPs, meet in the Scottish Parliament building at Holyrood in Edinburgh .

question_en: The UK continues to be a world leader in the development and manufacture of motor-sport technology:
explanation_en: There is a long history of motor sport in the UK, for both cars and motor cycles. Motor-car racing in the UK started in 1902. The UK continues to be a world leader in the development and manufacture of motor-sport technology.

question_en: Why was the ‘Habeas Corpus Act’ of 1679 an important piece of legislation?

options.en: It allowed people over 21 years old to vote
It guaranteed that no one could be held prisoner unlawfully
It abolished slavery throughout the British Empire
It abolished a number of taxes on imported goods in the UK

explanation_en: The Habeas Corpus Act became law in 1679. This was a very important piece of legislation which remains relevant today. The Act guaranteed that no one could be held prisoner unlawfully. Every prisoner has a right to a court hearing.

question_en: What British artist was awarded the Turner Prize?
explanation_en: Damien Hirst .

question_en: How many members has the Council of Europe?
explanation_en: The Council of Europe is separated from the EU. It has 47 member countries , including the UK, and is responsible for the protection and promotion of human rights in those countries.

question_en: In the UK, Members of the Parliament (MPs) are elected on the basis of:

options.en: First past the post system (the candidate who gets the most votes)

explanation_en: MPs are elected through a system called ‘first past the post’ . In  constituency, the candidate who gets the most votes is elected.

question_en: Which of the following statements regarding television in Northern Ireland is TRUE?

options.en: Some programmes are broadcasted in Irish Gaelic
All programmes are broadcasted in English
There are no specific programmes to Northern Ireland

explanation_en: There are programmes specific to Northern Ireland and some programmes broadcast in Irish Gaelic.

question_en: In the second half of the 19th century there was an important group of artists who painted detailed pictures on religious or literary themes in bright colours. These were known as:
explanation_en: The Pre-Raphaelites were an important group of artists in the second half of the 19th century. They painted detailed pictures on religious or literary themes in bright colours. The group included Holman Hunt, Dante Gabriel Rossetti and Sir John Millais.

question_en: The Muslim festival known as Eid ul Adha reminds Muslims of their own commitment to God:
explanation_en: Eid ul Adha reminds Muslims of their own commitment to God.

question_en: What was the population of the UK in 1901?
explanation_en: The population of the UK in 1901 was 40 million people .

question_en: People in the UK do NOT have to pay tax on:
explanation_en: People in the UK have to pay tax on their income, which includes: wages from paid employment, profits from self-employment, taxable benefits, pensions and income from property, savings and dividends.

question_en: Which British scientist co-invented the MRI (magnetic resonance imaging) scanner?
explanation_en: Sir Peter Mansfield (1933-), a British scientist, is the co-inventor of the MRI (magnetic resonance imaging) scanner. This enables doctors and researchers to obtain exact and non-invasive images of human internal organs and has revolutionised diagnostic medicine.

question_en: Which political party called a referendum on the UK’s membership of the European Union?
explanation_en: The Conservative government called a referendum on the UK’s membership of the European Union.

question_en: Which of the following is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and a popular area for walkers?
explanation_en: Parts of Hadrian’s Wall , including the forts of Housesteads and Vindolanda is a popular area for walkers and is a UNESCO (United Nations Educational Scientific and Cultural Organization) World Heritage Site.

question_en: When did hereditary peers lose the automatic right to attend the House of Lords?
explanation_en: Since 1999 , hereditary peers have lost the automatic right to attend the House of Lords. They now elect a few of their number to represent them in the House of Lords.

question_en: Which charity works for the preservation of buildings in England?
explanation_en: In the UK, many parts of the countryside and places of interest are kept open by the National Trust in England, Wales and Northern Ireland and the National Trust for Scotland. Both are charities that work to preserve important buildings, coastline and countryside in the UK.

question_en: What name is given to the new social classes that appeared after the Black Death plague and who owned large areas of land?
explanation_en: Following the Black Death, the smaller population meant there was less need to grow cereal crops. There were labour shortages and peasants began to demand higher wages. New social classes appeared, including owners of large areas of land (later called the gentry ), and people left the countryside to live in the towns. In the towns, growing wealth led to the development of a strong middle class.

question_en: Which of the following is a traditional Welsh food?
explanation_en: Welsh cakes are a traditional Welsh snack made from flour, dried fruits and spices, and served either hot or cold.

question_en: Which of the following was one of the most famous battles of the Hundred Years War?
explanation_en: One of the most famous battles of the Hundred Years War was the Battle of Agincourt in 1415, where King Henry V’s vastly outnumbered English army defeated the French. The English left France in the 1450s.

question_en: During the Middle Ages, England was an important trading nation and people came to England from abroad to trade and also to work. Where did the engineers come from?
explanation_en: During the Middle Ages, people came to England from abroad to trade and also to work. Many had special skills, such as weavers from France, engineers from Germany , glass manufacturers from Italy and canal builders from Holland.

question_en: Who was the captain of the English football team that won the World Cup in 1966?
explanation_en: Bobby Moore (1941-93) captained the English football team that won the World Cup in 1966.

question_en: Membership to the Commonwealth  is compulsory for its members:
explanation_en: Membership to the Commonwealth is voluntary.

question_en: How many verdicts are possible in trials in Scotland?
explanation_en: The jury has to listen to the evidence presented at the trial and then decide a verdict of ‘guilty’ or ‘not guilty’ based of what they have heard. In Scotland, a third verdict of ‘not proven’ is also possible.

question_en: How many wives did Henry VIII have?
explanation_en: Henry VIII had six wives .

question_en: Which of the following charities works with sick animals?
explanation_en: The PDSA is the People’s Dispensary for Sick Animals.

question_en: Which of the following is the responsibility of the Home Secretary?
explanation_en: The Home Secretary is responsible for crime, policing and immigration .

question_en: Which of the following sports can be traced as far back as the Roman times?
explanation_en: There is a very long history of horse racing in Britain, with evidence of events taking place as far back as Roman times.

question_en: When did the English become the preferred language of the royal court and Parliament?
explanation_en: By 1400 , in England, official documents were being written in English, and English had become the preferred language of the royal court and Parliament.

question_en: When is St Andrews Day, patron saint of Scotland?
explanation_en: St Andrews Day, patron saint of Scotland, is celebrated on the 30th of November .

question_en: Which organisation has been organising ‘The Proms’ since 1927?
explanation_en: The Proms is an eight-week summer season of orchestral classical music that takes place in various venues, including the Royal Albert Hall in London. It has been in various venues, including the Royal Albert Hall in London. It has been organised by the British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC) since 1927.

question_en: Which of the following organisations has no power to make laws but draws up conventions and charters, including the European Convention on Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms?
explanation_en: The Council of Europe is responsible for the protection and promotion of human rights in those countries. It has no power to make laws but draws up conventions and charters, the most well-known of which is the European Convention on Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms, usually called the European Convention on Human Rights.

question_en: Where is Snowdonia National Park located?
explanation_en: Snowdonia is a national park in North Wales .

question_en: What actions did Henry VII take after his victory in the Wars of the Roses?

options.en: Reduced the power of the nobles
Broke away with the Church of Rome
Increased the power of the nobles

explanation_en: After his victory in the Wars of the Roses, Henry VII wanted to make sure that England remained peaceful and that his position as king was secure. He deliberately strengthened the central administration of England and reduced the power of the nobles.

question_en: Which Court deals with the most serious civil cases in Scotland?
explanation_en: More serious civil cases – for example, when a large amount of compensation is being claimed – are dealt with in the High Court of England, Wales and Northern Ireland. In Scotland, they are dealt with in the Court of Session in Edinburgh.

question_en: The leader of the opposition leads his or her party in pointing out what they see as the government’s (choose TWO options):
explanation_en: The leader of the opposition leads his or her party in pointing out what they see as the government’s failures and weaknesses .

question_en: Who became Prime Minister of the UK in May 2010?
explanation_en: In May 2010, and for the first time in the UK since February 1974, no political party won an overall majority in the General Election. The Conservative and Liberal Democrat parties formed a coalition and the leader of the Conservative Party, David Cameron , became Prime Minister.

question_en: The Reform Act of 1832 abolished the old pocket and rotten boroughs.
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: What is the minimum age requirement to drive a moped in the UK?
explanation_en: You need to be at least 16 years old to ride a moped, and there are other age requirements and special tests for driving large vehicles.

question_en: Which of the following statements is true?

options.en: Great Britain refers only to England, Wales, Northern Ireland, not to Scotland.
Great Britain refers only to England, Northern Ireland and Scotland, not to Wales.
Great Britain refers to England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland.
Great Britain refers only to England, Scotland and Wales, not to Northern Ireland.

explanation_en: Great Britain refers only to England, Scotland and Wales, not to Northern Ireland.

question_en: How many countries formed the European Economic Community (EEC) when it was first created in 1957?
explanation_en: The European Economic Community (EEC), was set up by six western European countries (Belgium, France, Germany, Italy, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) who signed the Treaty of Rome on 25 March 1957.

question_en: Northern Ireland and Wales have their own banknotes, which are valid everywhere in the UK. However, shop and businesses do not have to accept them:
explanation_en: Northern Ireland and Scotland have their own banknotes, which are valid everywhere in the UK. However, shops and businesses do not have to accept them.

question_en: What is donated blood used for?

options.en: To help people with a wide range of injuries and illnesses
Blood cannot be donated in the UK
To carry out research with your DNA
To be stored in the hospital in case you need it in the future

explanation_en: Donated blood is used by hospitals to help people with a wide range of injuries and illnesses .

question_en: How often are the general elections held in the UK?
explanation_en: MPs are elected at a General Election, which is held at least every five years .

question_en: Where is the Grand National horse racing event celebrated?
explanation_en: The Grand National horse racing event is celebrated in Aintree near Liverpool.

question_en: What was Charles Chaplin famous for?
explanation_en: Sir Charles (Charlie) Chaplin became famous in silent movies for his tramp character and was one of many British actors to make a career in Hollywood.

question_en: What was Boudicca, the queen of the Iceni in what is now eastern England, known for?
explanation_en: One of the tribal leaders who fought against the Romans was Boudicca, the queen of the Iceni in what is now eastern England.

question_en: What percentage of the British population has a parent or grandparent born outside of the UK as a result of post-war immigration?
explanation_en: Post-war immigration means that nearly 10% of the population has a parent or grandparent born outside the UK.

question_en: When was the hovercraft invented?
explanation_en: Sir Christopher Cockerell (1910-99), a British inventor, invented the hovercraft in the 1950s .

question_en: Who is the author of the famous play ‘Macbeth’?
explanation_en: Macbeth is a famous play by William Shakespeare .

question_en: What animal was cloned by scientists Sir Ian Wilmot and Keith Campbell?
explanation_en: In 1996, two British scientists, Sir Ian Wilmot (1944-) and Keith Campbell (1954-2012), led a team which was the first to succeed in cloning a mammal, Dolly the sheep .

question_en: What is the name of the building where the Northern Ireland Assembly meet?
explanation_en: The Northern Ireland Assembly building is known as Stormont .

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a function of the House of Lords?

options.en: Check laws that have been passed by the House of Commons

explanation_en: The House of Lords can suggest amendments or propose new laws, which are then discussed by MPs. The House of Lords checks laws that have been passed by the House of Commons to ensure they are fit for purpose. It also holds the government to account to make sure that it is working in the best interests of the people.

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a World Heritage Site?

options.en: The forts of Housesteads and Vindolanda

explanation_en: The London Eye is not a World Heritage Site.

question_en: Which famous murder-mystery play has been running in the west end since 1952 and has had the longest initial run of any show in history?
explanation_en: The Mousetrap , a murder-mystery play by Dame Agatha Christie, has been running in the west end since 1952 and has had the longest initial run of any show in history.

question_en: When was the Giant’s Causeway formed?
explanation_en: The Giant’s Causeway was formed about 50 million years ago .

question_en: Who was the first person to sail single-handed around the world without stopping?
explanation_en: A British sailor, Sir Francis Chichester, was the first person to sail singlehanded around the world, in 1966/67. Two years later, Sir Robin Knox- Johnston became the first person to do this without stopping.

question_en: What British writer created the fictional detective Sherlock Holmes?
explanation_en: Sir Arthur Conan Doyle was a Scottish doctor and writer. He was best known for his stories about Sherlock Holmes, who was one of the first fictional detectives.

question_en: How can you visit the  Senedd?
explanation_en: To book guided tours or seats in the public galleries for the Senedd you can contact the Senedd Booking Service on 0300 200 6565, email contact@senedd.wales or visit www.senedd.wales for an online booking form.

question_en: How were Elizabeth I and ‘Mary, Queen of Scots’ related?
explanation_en: ‘Mary, Queen of Scots’ was Elizabeth I’s cousin.

question_en: Choose the correct answer. The Middle Ages were:

options.en: A period of peace in Britain
A period where liberal ideas were introduced in Britain
A period where new ideas about politics and philosophy were introduced in Britain

explanation_en: The Middle Ages (or the medieval period) was a time of almost constant war .

question_en: What does the NATO stand for?
explanation_en: NATO stands for The North Atlantic Treaty Organisation .

question_en: What characterised the people from the Bronze Age?

options.en: They were farmers who lived in large villages

explanation_en: The people of the Bronze Age were accomplished metalworkers who made many beautiful objects in bronze and gold, including tools, ornaments and weapons.

question_en: Where is the National Horseracing Museum located?
explanation_en: The National Horseracing Museum is located in Newmarket, Suffolk .

question_en: In which of the following matters the Scottish Parliament CANNOT legislate?
explanation_en: The matters on which the Scottish Parliament can legislate include: civil and criminal law, health, education, planning and additional tax-raising powers.

question_en: Which of the following is a popular showcase of mainly theatre and comedy performances and the biggest and most well-known festival that takes place during the Edinburgh Festival?
explanation_en: The Fringe is a showcase of mainly theatre and comedy performances. It often shows experimental work.The biggest and most well-known being the Edinburgh Festival Fringe.

question_en: Does Britain have a constitutional monarchy?
explanation_en: The UK has a constitutional monarchy.

question_en: Which Scottish city is known as the home of golf?
explanation_en: St. Andrews in Scotland is known as the home of golf.

question_en: Which court deals with the most serious cases of children aged 10 to 17 in England, Wales and Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland, if an accused person is aged 10 to 17, the case is normally heard in a Youth Court in front of up to three specially trained magistrates or a District Judge. The most serious cases will go the Crown Court .

question_en: What is the name of the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe and which origin traces back to the Stone Age?
explanation_en: Skara Brae on Orkney, off the north coast of Scotland, is the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe, and has helped archaeologists to understand more about how people lived near the end of the Stone Age.

question_en: When was the National Trust founded?
explanation_en: The National Trust was founded in 1895 by three volunteers.

question_en: After the Black Death, new social classes appeared in England, including owners of large areas of land known as:
explanation_en: Following the Black Death, the smaller population meant there was less need to grow cereal crops. There were labour shortages and peasants began to demand higher wages. New social classes appeared, including owners of large areas of land (later called the gentry ), and people left the countryside to live in the towns. In the towns, growing wealth led to the development of a strong middle class.

question_en: Elizabeth I was the younger daughter of which King?
explanation_en: Elizabeth I was the younger daughter of Henry VIII .

question_en: Which of the following operas was written by Gilbert and Sullivan?
explanation_en: In the 19th century, Gilbert and Sullivan wrote comic operas, often making fun of popular culture and politics. These operas include HMS Pinafore, The Pirates of Penzance and The Mikado .

question_en: Where is Loch Lomond and the Trossachs National Park located?
explanation_en: Loch Lomond and the Trossachs National Park is located in the west of Scotland .

question_en: Who is married to the King of the United Kingdom?
explanation_en: The King of the United Kingdom is married to Camila, the Queen Consort.

question_en: By what TWO other names is the Church of England known?
explanation_en: The official Church of the state is the Church of England (called the Anglican Church in other countries and the Episcopal Church in Scotland and the United States).

question_en: Who was the first British Prime Minister?
explanation_en: The first man to British Prime Minister was Sir Robert Walpole , who was Prime Minister from 1721 to 1742.

question_en: What day does Lent start?
explanation_en: Lent begins on Ash Wednesday.

question_en: How old do you need to be to apply for a free TV licence?
explanation_en: People over 75 can apply for a free TV licence and blind people can get a 50% discount.

question_en: What time do pubs usually open on Sundays in the UK?
explanation_en: Pubs are usually open during the day from 11.00 am (12 noon on Sundays).

question_en: What is the official home of the Prime Minister?
explanation_en: The official home of the Prime Minister is 10 Downing Street, in central London, near the Houses of Parliament. He or she also has a country house outside London called Chequers.

question_en: When did the Conservative government called a referendum on the UK’s membership of the European Union?
explanation_en: The Conservative government called a referendum on the UK’s membership of the European Union on the 23rd June 2016.

question_en: Which British sportswoman won two gold medals for running in the 2004 Olympic Games?
explanation_en: Dame Kelly Holmes won two gold medals for running in the 2004 Olympic Games. She has held a number of British and European records.

question_en: What percentage of the total British population is located in England alone?
explanation_en: The population is very unequally distributed over the four parts of the UK. England more or less consistently makes up 84% of the total population, Wales around 5%, Scotland just over 8%, and Northern Ireland less than 3%.

question_en: What was the population of the UK in 2010?
explanation_en: The population of the UK in 2010 was just over 62 million people.

question_en: Which court deals with cases involving personal injury, family matters, breaches of contract and divorce in England and Wales?
explanation_en: County Courts deal with a wide range of civil disputes. These include people trying to get back money that is owed to them, cases involving personal injury, family matters, breaches of contract, and divorce. In Scotland, most of these matters are dealt with in the Sheriff Court.

question_en: What is the name of the building where the Scottish Parliament meet?
explanation_en: In Scotland the elected members, called MSPs, meet in the Scottish Parliament building at Holyrood in Edinburgh.

question_en: Which flower is associated with England?
explanation_en: The rose is the flower associated with England.

question_en: Who was Robert Burns?
explanation_en: Known in Scotland as ‘The Bard’, Robert Burns was a Scottish poet .

question_en: In which period did British Film studios flourish?
explanation_en: British film studios flourished in the 1930s.

question_en: Who defeated the French at the battle of Agincourt in 1415?
explanation_en: One of the most famous battles of the Hundred Years War was the Battle of Agincourt in 1415, where King Henry V ’s vastly outnumbered English army defeated the French.

question_en: Which is the most famous tennis tournament hosted in Britain and which takes place each year at the All England Lawn Tennis and Croquet Club?
explanation_en: The most famous tournament hosted in Britain is The Wimbledon Championships , which takes place each year at the All England Lawn Tennis and Croquet Club.

question_en: What is the minimum age required for a citizen to stand for public office?
explanation_en: Most citizens of the UK, the Irish republic or the Commonwealth aged 18 or over can stand for public office.

question_en: Who makes sure that the opposition has a guaranteed amount of time to debate issues which it chooses during political debates?
explanation_en: The Speaker keeps order during political debates to make sure the rules are followed. This includes making sure the opposition has a guaranteed amount of time to debate issues which it chooses.

question_en: Citizens of Liechtenstein can drive in the UK for as long as their driving license is valid:
explanation_en: If your driving licence if from a country in the European Union (EU), Iceland, Liechtenstein or Norway, you can drive in the UK for as long as your licence is valid.

question_en: Which British Prime Minister nationalized major industries including coal and steel?
explanation_en: Clement Attlee ’s government undertook the nationalisation of major industries (like coal and steel), created the National Health Service and implemented many of Beveridge’s plans for a stronger welfare state. Attlee also introduced measures to improve the conditions of workers.

question_en: Who was the first Archbishop of Canterbury?
