question_en: What is the name of the official reports where the Parliament’s proceedings are published?
explanation_en: Proceedings in Parliament are broadcast on television and published in official reports called Hansard .

question_en: How many members does the Senedd have?
explanation_en: The Senedd has 60 Senedd members (SMs) and elections are held every four years using a form of proportional representation.

question_en: Who deals with cases involving personal injury, family matters, breaches of contract and divorce in Scotland?
explanation_en: Civil disputes such as people trying to get back money that is owed to them, cases involving personal injury, family matters, breaches of contract, and divorce are dealt with in the Sheriff Court in Scotland.

question_en: What name is given to the members of the House of Lords?
explanation_en: Members of the House of Lords are known as peers .

question_en: Magistrates and Justices of the Peace (JPs) are members of:
explanation_en: Magistrates and Justices of the Peace (JPs) are members of the local community .

question_en: How can you visit the Northern Ireland Assembly (choose TWO options)?

options.en: Arranging a tour through the visitor services

explanation_en: There are two ways to arrange a visit to the Northern Ireland Assembly at Stormont. You can either contact the Education Service (details are on the Northern Ireland Assembly website at http://www.niassembly.gov.uk) or contact an MLA .

question_en: For what sort of claims is the small claims procedure in Northern Ireland used?
explanation_en: The small claims procedure is used for claims of less than £10,000 in England and Wales and less than £3,000 in Scotland and Northern Ireland .

question_en: Which TWO of the following charities work with homeless people?
explanation_en: ﻿harities working with homeless people include Shelter and Crisis .

question_en: Members of the Senedd (SMs) are elected every four years on the basis of:

options.en: First past the post system (the candidate who gets the most votes)

explanation_en: The Senedd has 60 Senedd members (SMs) and elections are held every four years using a form of proportional representation .

question_en: What is the Commonwealth?

options.en: An association of countries that support each other and work together towards shared goals in democracy and development
An organization responsible for the protection and promotion of human rights in its member countries
An association of countries that promotes international peace and security
A group of European and North American countries that have agreed to help each other if they come under attack

explanation_en: The Commonwealth is an association of countries that support each other and work together towards shared goals in democracy and development .

question_en: When was the voting age reduced to 18 for men and women?
explanation_en: In 1969 , the voting age was reduced to 18 for men and women.

question_en: When was the Northern Ireland Parliament established for the first time?
explanation_en: The Northern Ireland Parliament established for the first time in 1922 .

question_en: What is the opposition?

options.en: The second largest party in the House of Lords
The third largest party in the House of Commons
The second largest party in the House of Commons
The third largest party in the House of Lords

explanation_en: The second largest party in the House of Commons is called the opposition.

question_en: Which THREE of the following groups form part of the British government?
explanation_en: In the UK, there are several different parts of government. The main ones are: the monarchy, the Parliament (the House of Commons and the House of Lords), the Prime Minister, the cabinet, the judiciary (courts), the police, the civil service and the local government .

question_en: Who chairs the debates in the House of Commons?
explanation_en: Debates in the House of Commons are chaired by the Speaker .

question_en: A woman who is violent towards her partner cannot be prosecuted in the UK:
explanation_en: False . Anyone who is violent towards their partner – whether they are a man or a woman, married or living together – can be prosecuted.

question_en: Many MPs, SMs and MSPs hold regular local events where constituents can go in person to talk about issues that are of concern to them. These are known as:
explanation_en: Many MPs, SMs and MSPs hold regular local ‘surgeries’ , where constituents can go in person to talk about issues that are of concern to them.

question_en: Which Act introduced the Forced Marriage Protection Orders for England, Wales and Northern Ireland in 2008?
explanation_en: Forced Marriage Protection Orders were introduced in 2008 for England, Wales and Northern Ireland under the Forced Marriage (Civil Protection) Act 2007 . Similar Protection Orders were introduced in Scotland in November 2011.

question_en: When is the electoral register updated?
explanation_en: The electoral register is updated every year in September or October .

question_en: Where does the Scottish Parliament sit?
explanation_en: In Scotland the elected members, called MSPs, meet in the Scottish Parliament building at Holyrood in Edinburgh .

question_en: Which of the following is a ceremonial role to be undertaken by the King?

options.en: Opening the new parliamentary session every year
Visiting the major cities in the UK once every six months
Giving a speech on New Year’s Eve

explanation_en: The King has important ceremonial roles, such as the opening of the new parliamentary session each year . On this occasion the King makes a speech which summarises the government’s policies for the year ahead.

question_en: How long can you use your driving licence in the UK if you have a licence from another country?

options.en: You need a UK driving licence
For as long as it is valid

explanation_en: If you have a licence from another country, you may use it in the UK for up to 12 months .

question_en: Which Court deals with the most serious civil cases in England, Wales and Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: More serious civil cases – for example, when a large amount of compensation is being claimed – are dealt with in the High Court of England, Wales and Northern Ireland.

question_en: If you are arrested and taken to a police station, a police officer should:

options.en: Make a copy of your ID and retain you until your lawyer arrives
Tell you the reason for your arrest and that you will be able to seek legal advice
Offer you a cup of tea
Explain the law to you and wait for your family to arrive

explanation_en: If you are arrested and taken to a police station, a police officer will tell you the reason for your arrest and you will be able to seek legal advice .

question_en: The UK is one of the five permanent members of the Security Council:
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: Which of the following four changes did the Chartists NOT campaign for?

options.en: For any man to be able to stand as MP

explanation_en: The Chartists campaigned for every man to have the vote, elections every year , for all regions to be equal in the electoral system, secret ballots, for any man to be able to stand as an MP and for MPs to be paid.

question_en: Which of the following is a criminal offence?

options.en: Selling tobacco to someone under the age of 18

explanation_en: It is illegal and a criminal offence to sell tobacco products (for example, cigarettes, cigars, roll-up tobacco) to anyone under the age of 18 .

question_en: Which court deals with serious criminal offences in Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland, serious criminal offences are tried in front of a judge and a jury in a Crown Court .

question_en: Which political party does the Speaker represent?

options.en: The Speaker does not represent any political party

explanation_en: The Speaker is neutral and does not represent a political party , even though he or she is an MP, represents a constituency and deals with the constituents’ problems like any other MP.

question_en: When was the Scottish Parliament formed?
explanation_en: The Scottish Parliament was formed in 1999 .

question_en: Who is the heir to the throne of the UK?
explanation_en: The King’s eldest son, Prince William (the Prince of Wales) , is the heir to the throne.

question_en: Membership to the Commonwealth is compulsory for its members:
explanation_en: False . Membership to the Commonwealth is voluntary .

question_en: How many verdicts are possible in trials in Scotland?
explanation_en: The jury has to listen to the evidence presented at the trial and then decide a verdict of ‘guilty’ or ‘not guilty’ based of what they have heard. In Scotland, a third verdict of ‘not proven’ is also possible.

question_en: Which of the following charities works with sick animals?
explanation_en: The PDSA is the People’s Dispensary for Sick Animals.

question_en: Which of the following is the responsibility of the Home Secretary?
explanation_en: The Home Secretary is responsible for crime, policing and immigration .

question_en: What is the minimum age required for a citizen to stand for public office?
explanation_en: Most citizens of the UK, the Irish republic or the Commonwealth aged 18 or over can stand for public office.

question_en: Who makes sure that the opposition has a guaranteed amount of time to debate issues which it chooses during political debates?
explanation_en: The Speaker keeps order during political debates to make sure the rules are followed. This includes making sure the opposition has a guaranteed amount of time to debate issues which it chooses.

question_en: Citizens of Liechtenstein can drive in the UK for as long as their driving license is valid:
explanation_en: True . If your driving licence if from a country in the European Union (EU), Iceland, Liechtenstein or Norway, you can drive in the UK for as long as your licence is valid.

question_en: What is the money raised from the Income Tax used for (choose THREE options)?
explanation_en: Money raised from income tax pays for government services such as roads, education, police and the armed forces .

question_en: When were the Forced Marriage Protection Orders introduced for England, Wales and Northern Ireland, which aimed at protecting a person from being forced into a marriage, or to protect a person in a forced marriage?
explanation_en: Forced Marriage Protection Orders were introduced in 2008 for England, Wales and Northern Ireland under the Forced Marriage (Civil Protection) Act 2007.

question_en: In the UK, if the jury finds a defendant guilty, the judge decides the penalty:
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: Which of the following countries is a member of the Commonwealth?
explanation_en: Bangladesh is a member of the Commonwealth.

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a principle included in the European Convention of Human Rights?

options.en: Prohibition of slavery and forced labour

explanation_en: Prohibition of  immoral thoughts is not one of the principles included in the European Convention of Human Rights.

question_en: A system of government where the whole adult population gets a say is known as:
explanation_en: Democracy is a system of government where the whole adult population gets a say. This might be by direct voting or by choosing representatives to make decisions on their behalf.

question_en: Which of the following statements is correct?

options.en: Arranged marriages refer to marriages where both parties agree to the marriage
Arranged marriages refer to marriages where one or both parties do not give their consent to enter into the partnership

explanation_en: Arranged marriages refer to marriages where both parties agree to the marriage.

question_en: The devolved governments in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland have the power to legislate on any issues that affect them:
explanation_en: False . Devolved governments in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland have the power to legislate on certain issues .

question_en: What name is given to the members of the House of Commons?
explanation_en: All of the elected MPs form the House of Commons.

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a role of the King:

options.en: To inaugurate important business in the UK
To receive foreign ambassadors and high commissioners
To entertain visiting heads of state

explanation_en: The King receives foreign ambassadors and high commissioners, entertains visiting heads of state, and makes state visits overseas in support of diplomatic and relationships with other counties.

question_en: The Speaker is a neutral MP and does not represent a political party:
explanation_en: True . The Speaker is neutral and does not represent a political party, even though he or she is an MP, represents a constituency and deals with the constituents’ problems like any other MP.

question_en: How can a person facing domestic violence find a safe place to stay (choose TWO options)?

options.en: Going to a hotel away from their home
Contacting the National Domestic Violence Freephone Helpline

explanation_en: If you are facing domestic violence you can get help from the 24-hour National Domestic Violence Freephone Helpline on 0808 2000 247 at any time, or the police can help you find a safe place to stay.

question_en: Who has control over policy and laws governing defence, foreign affairs, immigration, taxation and social security in Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: Policy and laws governing defence, foreign affairs, immigration, taxation and social security all remain under central UK government control. However, many other public services, such as education, are controlled by the devolved administrations.

question_en: The devolved governments in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland cannot control policies and laws governing taxation and social security:
explanation_en: True . Policy and laws governing defence, foreign affairs, immigration, taxation and social security all remain under central UK government control. However, many other public services, such as education, are controlled by the devolved administrations.

question_en: In the UK, brutality and violence in the home is not a crime:
explanation_en: False . In the UK, brutality and violence in the home is a serious crime .

question_en: UK laws ensure that people are not treated unfairly in any area of life or work because of their age, disability, sex, pregnancy and maternity, race, religion or belief, sexuality or marital status:
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: Since 1997, some powers have been devolved from the central government to give people in Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland more control over matters that directly affect them:
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: Which of the following territories does not belong to Great Britain?
explanation_en: ‘Great Britain’ refers only to England, Scotland and Wales, not to Northern Ireland. The official name of the country is the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland.

question_en: Who was the first Briton to win the ‘Tour de France’?
explanation_en: Bradley Wiggins became the first Briton to win the ‘Tour de France’.

question_en: Which of the following statements is TRUE?

options.en: The English language has no dialects
The English language has many accents and dialects
Gaelic is spoken in North Wales
In Northern Ireland people speak French

explanation_en: The English language has many accents and dialects.

question_en: How old do you need to be to go into betting shops or gambling clubs?
explanation_en: In the UK, you have to be 18 to go into betting shops or gambling clubs.

question_en: What do Ian McEwan, Hilary Mantel and Julian Barnes have in common?

options.en: They were awarded with the Man Booker Prize for Fiction
They won a Nobel Prize in Literature
They were awarded with the Mercury Music Prize

explanation_en: The Man Booker Prize for Fiction is awarded annually for the best fiction novel written by an author from the Commonwealth, Ireland or Zimbabwe. It has been awarded since 1968. Past winners include Ian McEwan, Hilary Mantel and Julian Barnes.

question_en: In Northern Ireland members of the Legislative Assembly are elected on the basis of:
explanation_en: The Northern Ireland Assembly has 90 elected members, known as MLAs (members of the Legislative Assembly). They are elected with a form of proportional representation.

question_en: How can MPs be contacted (choose TWO options)?

options.en: By going to your local council and asking for an appointment

explanation_en: You can contact MPs by letter or telephone at their constituency office, or at their office in the House of Commons: The House of Commons, Westminster, London SW1A OAA, telephone 0207729 3000.

question_en: What did the’Bill of Rights’ confirm in 1689?

options.en: The rights of Parliament and the limits of the king’s power
The supreme power of the king

explanation_en: The Bill of Rights, 1689, confirmed the rights of Parliament and the limits of the king’s power. Parliament took control of who could be monarch and declared that the king or queen must be a Protestant.

question_en: Who designed the Clifton Suspension Bridge located over the Avon George?
explanation_en: The Clifton Suspension Bridge was designed by Isambard Kingdom Brunel.

question_en: Does Britain have a written constitution?
explanation_en: The British constitution is not written down in any single document, and therefore it is described as ‘unwritten’. This is mainly because the UK, unlike America or France, has never had a revolution which led permanently to a totally new system of government.

question_en: What was the estimated population of the British Empire during the Victorian period?
explanation_en: During the Victorian period, the British Empire grew to cover all of India, Australia and large parts of Africa. It became the largest empire the world has ever seen, with an estimated population of more than 400 million people.

question_en: Which of the following countries was NOT part of the Allied Powers during the First World War?
explanation_en: Bulgaria formed part of the Central Powers during the First World War.

question_en: How many times have the Olympic Games been hosted in the UK?
explanation_en: The UK has hosted the Olympic games on three occasions: 1908, 1948 and 2012.

question_en: When were films first shown publicly in the UK?
explanation_en: Films were first shown publicly in the UK in 1896 and film screenings very quickly became popular.

question_en: What is the name of the building where the Welsh government members meet?
explanation_en: Elected members of the Welsh government meet at the Senedd in Cardiff, the capital city of Wales.

question_en: Where is the city of Southampton located?
explanation_en: Southampton is located in England.

question_en: When did the First World War finish?
explanation_en: The First World War ended at 11.00 am on 11th November 1918 with victory for Britain and its allies.

question_en: Florence Nightingale was known for her work on:

options.en: The development of nursing in the UK
The development and manufacture of motor-sport technology

explanation_en: Florence Nightingale is often regarded as the founder of modern nursing.

question_en: Which Act abolished slavery throughout the British Empire in 1833?
explanation_en: In 1833 the Emancipation Act abolished slavery throughout the British Empire. The Royal Navy stopped slave ships from other countries, freed the slaves and punished the slave traders.

question_en: How long does Diwali last for?
explanation_en: Diwali normally falls in October or November and lasts for five days.

question_en: Who became King of Britain when Queen Anne died in 1714?
explanation_en: When Queen Anne died in 1714, Parliament chose a German, George I, to be the next king, because he was Anne’s nearest Protestant relative.

question_en: What was the population of the UK in 2005?
explanation_en: The population of the UK in 2005 was just under 60 million people.

question_en: When did the ‘Habeas Corpus Act’ become law?
explanation_en: The Habeas Corpus Act became law in 1679.

question_en: Who discovered the penicillin in 1928?
explanation_en: Alexander Fleming was researching influenza (the ‘flu’) in 1928 when he discovered penicillin.

question_en: During the Middle Ages, England was an important trading nation and people came to England from abroad to trade and also to work. Where did glass manufacturers come from?
explanation_en: During the Middle Ages people came to England from abroad to trade and also to work, such as weavers from France, engineers from Germany, glass manufacturers from Italy and canal builders from Holland.

question_en: Where can people facing domestic violence get help from?
explanation_en: People facing domestic violence can get help from a solicitor or the Citizens Advice Bureau .

question_en: Which American President worked closely with Margaret Thatcher?
explanation_en: Margaret Thatcher worked closely with the United States President, Ronald Reagan , and was one of the first Western leaders to recognise and welcome the changes in the leadership of the Soviet Union which eventually led to the end of the Cold War.

question_en: People might be taken to court if they owe money to someone.
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: Who was the first Archbishop of Canterbury?
explanation_en: St Augustine led missionaries from Rome, who spread Christianity in the south. St Augustine became the first Archbishop of Canterbury.

question_en: How many members does a jury have in England, Wales and Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland a jury has 12 members , and in Scotland a jury has 15 members.

question_en: What is tested with a driving test (choose TWO options)?
explanation_en: To get a UK driving licence you must pass a driving test, which tests both your knowledge and your practical skills.

question_en: What is the role of the Speaker?

options.en: To keep order during political debates to make sure the rules are followed
To point out what they see as the government’s failures and weaknesses
To challenge the government and put forward alternative policies

explanation_en: The Speaker keeps order during political debates to make sure the rules are followed.

question_en: Which of the following statements is FALSE regarding Youth Courts?

options.en: The parents or carers of the young person being judged are expected to attend the hearing
Members of the public are not allowed in them
They are used for trials of young people aged 10 to 18
The name or photographs of the accused young person cannot be published in newspapers or used by the media

explanation_en: They are used for trials of young people aged 10 to 17.

question_en: Which TWO films were directed by David Lean?
explanation_en: David Lean directed Brief Encounter (1945) and Lawrence of Arabia (1962).

question_en: Which of the following venues is located in Glasgow?
explanation_en: The Scottish Exhibition and Conference Centre (SEC Centre) is located in Glasgow.

question_en: How many British casualties were recorded during the first day of the British attack of the Somme in July 1916 during the First World War?
explanation_en: The British attack of the Somme in July 1916, resulted in about 60,000 British casualties on the first day alone.

question_en: The Welsh dragon on the Welsh flag does not appear on the Union Flag because, when the first Union Flag was created in 1606 from the flags of Scotland and England, the Principality of Wales was already untied with England:
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: Which two countries developed the ‘Concorde’ aircraft?
explanation_en: Britain and France developed the world’s only supersonic commercial airliner, Concorde.

question_en: What was the population of the UK in 1801?
explanation_en: The population of the UK in 1801 was 8 million people.

question_en: Where is there UK geographically located?

options.en: In the north east of Europe
In the south east of Europe
In the north west of Europe
In the south west of Europe

explanation_en: The UK is located in the north west of Europe .

question_en: What is the main function of the Council of Europe?

options.en: To protect and promote human rights
To recommend action when there are international crises and threats to peace

explanation_en: The Council of Europe is responsible for the protection and promotion of human rights in its member countries.

question_en: Sir Alfred Hitchcock was an important British film director
explanation_en: Eminent directors included Sir Alexander Korda and Sir Alfred Hitchcock, who later left for Hollywood and remained an important film director until his death in 1980.

question_en: Which Scottish physician and researcher co-discovered the insulin used to treat diabetes?
explanation_en: The Scottish physician and researcher John Macleod was the co-discoverer of insulin, used to treat diabetes.

question_en: Who was voted the greatest Briton of all time in 2002?
explanation_en: Winston Churchill in 2002 was voted the greatest Briton of all time by the public.

question_en: When is St George’s Day, patron of England?
explanation_en: St George’s Day, patron of England is celebrated on the 23rd of April .

question_en: Which TWO of the following responsibilities will you have as a permanent resident or citizen of the UK?

options.en: To respect and obey the law
To go to church on Sundays
To look after the area in which you live and the environment

explanation_en: Respect and obey the law and look after the area in which you live and the environment are the correct answers.

question_en: Which TWO of the following social laws were liberalised during the ‘Swinging Sixties’?
explanation_en: The law of abortion and the law of divorce were liberalised during the ‘Swinging Sixties’.

question_en: Who was the mother of Elizabeth I?
explanation_en: Anne Boleyn and Henry VIII had one daughter, Elizabeth I.

question_en: Who was the first wife of Henry VIII?
explanation_en: Catherine of Aragon was the first wife of Henry VIII.

question_en: Which of the following flags is not represented in the union flag?
explanation_en: The Welsh flag does not appear on the Union Flag because, when the first Union Flag was created in 1606 from the flags of Scotland and England, the Principality of Wales was already untied with England.

question_en: When does the Edinburgh Festival take place?
explanation_en: The Edinburgh Festival takes place in Edinburgh, Scotland, every summer .

question_en: When did ‘the Concorde’ aircraft fly for the first time?
explanation_en: The ‘Concorde’, the world’s only supersonic passenger aircraft, first flew in 1969 .

question_en: By which other name is the Conservative Party also known as?
explanation_en: The modern Conservative Party is still sometimes referred to as ‘The Tories’.

question_en: When was the Northern Ireland Parliament established for the first time?
explanation_en: The Northern Ireland Parliament was first established in 1922 .

question_en: What is the opposition?

options.en: The second largest party in the House of Lords
The third largest party in the House of Commons
The second largest party in the House of Commons
The third largest party in the House of Lords

explanation_en: The second-largest party in the House of Commons is called the opposition.

question_en: Which two languages combined form the basis of the English language?
explanation_en: After the Norman Conquest, the king and his noblemen had spoken Norman French and the peasants had continued to speak Anglo-Saxon. Gradually these two languages combined to become one English language.

question_en: Which THREE of the following are known to be main parts of the British government?
explanation_en: In the UK, there are several different parts of government. The main ones are: the monarchy, the Parliament (the House of Commons and the House of Lords),  the Prime Minister, the cabinet, the judiciary (courts), the police, the civil service and the local government.

question_en: When did Adolf Hitler come to power in Germany?
explanation_en: Adolf Hitler came to power in Germany in 1933 .

question_en: Who invented the hovercraft?
explanation_en: Sir Christopher Cockerell (1910-99), a British inventor, invented the hovercraft in the 1950s.

question_en: Which court deals with serious criminal offences in Wales?
explanation_en: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland, serious criminal offences are tried in front of a judge and a jury in a Crown Court. In Scotland, serious cases are heard in a Sheriff Court with either a sheriff or a sheriff with a jury.

question_en: What is the minimum age requirement to buy alcohol in a pub or night club in the UK?
explanation_en: To buy alcohol in a pub or night club you must be 18 or over , but people under that age may be allowed in some pubs with an adult.

question_en: What is the coin with the highest value in the UK?
explanation_en: The coin with the highest value in the UK is the £2 coin.

question_en: When were Protection Orders to protect a person from being forced into a marriage introduced in Scotland?
explanation_en: Forced Marriage Protection Orders were introduced in 2008 for England, Wales and Northern Ireland under the Forced Marriage (Civil Protection) Act 2007. Court orders can be obtained to protect a person from being forced into a marriage, or to protect a person in a forced marriage. Similar Protection Orders were introduced in Scotland in November 2011 .

question_en: Which TWO of the following are famous horse racing events in the UK?
explanation_en: Famous horse-racing events in the UK include Royal Ascot and the Grand National .

question_en: Which of the following events relates to the German bombing of London and other British cities at night time during the World War II?
explanation_en: The Blitz relates to the German bombing of London and other British cities at night time during the World War II

question_en: Who wrote an oratorio called ‘Messiah’, which is regularly sung by choirs at Easter time?
explanation_en: George Frederick Handel wrote an oratorio, Messiah, which is sung regularly by choirs, often at Easter time.

question_en: When did hereditary peers lose the automatic right to attend the House of Lords?
explanation_en: Since 1999 , hereditary peers have lost the automatic right to attend the House of Lords.

question_en: Which of the following is England’s largest national park?
explanation_en: The Lake District is England’s largest national park.

question_en: Which of the following social changes took place during the ‘Swinging Sixties’?

options.en: The position of women in the workplace was improved
Everyone could study in the University for free
People from the same gender were allowed to get married

explanation_en: The position of women in the workplace was improved during the ‘Swinging Sixties’.

question_en: Where was Emmeline Pankhurst born?
explanation_en: Emmeline Pankhurst was born in Manchester in 1858.

question_en: Which of the following is a traditional English food?
explanation_en: Roast beef is a traditional English food.

question_en: What are pantomimes based on?
explanation_en: Pantomimes are based on fairy stories and are light-hearted plays with music and comedy, enjoyed by family audiences.

question_en: What was inscribed in the first coins to be minted in Britain during the Iron Age?

options.en: The names of Iron Age Kings

explanation_en: The people of the Iron Age made the first coins to be minted in Britain, some inscribed with the names of Iron Age kings .

question_en: What name is given to the Church of England in Scotland and the United States?
explanation_en: The Church of England is called the Episcopal Church in Scotland and the United States.

question_en: Who directed the movie ‘Four Weddings and a Funeral’ in 1994?
explanation_en: Four Weddings and a Funeral (1994) was directed by Mike Newell .

question_en: What was the population of the UK in 1951?
explanation_en: The population of the UK in 1951 was 50 million people.

question_en: Which TWO religions celebrate Diwali?
explanation_en: Diwali is celebrated by Hindus and Sikhs . It normally falls in October or November and lasts for five days. It is also often called the Festival of Lights.

question_en: Which of the following statements is true?

options.en: Proceedings in Parliament are broadcast on television and published in official reports
Proceedings in Parliament are not broadcast on television but published in official reports
Proceedings in Parliament are broadcast on the radio and not published in official reports
Proceedings in Parliament are broadcast on the radio only

explanation_en: Proceedings in Parliament are broadcast on television and published in official reports called Hansard.

question_en: In Northern Ireland, a newly qualified driver must display an R plate for one year after passing the test.
explanation_en: This statement is TRUE .

question_en: When did Emmeline Pankhurst set up the women’s Franchise League, which fought to get the vote in local elections for married women?
explanation_en: Emmeline Pankhurst was born in Manchester in 1858. She set up the women’s Franchise League in 1889 , which fought to get the vote in local elections for married women. In 1903 she helped found the Women’s Social and Political Union (WSPU).

question_en: How many ski centres are there in Scotland?
explanation_en: There are five ski centres in Scotland, as well as Europe’s longest dry ski slope near Edinburgh.

question_en: The public can listen to debates in the Palace of Westminster from public galleries in the House of Commons but not in the House of Lords:
explanation_en: The public can listen to debates in the Palace of Westminster from public galleries in both the House of Commons and the House of Lords.

question_en: Which is the oldest tennis tournament in the world?
explanation_en: The most famous tournament hosted in Britain is The Wimbledon Championships , which takes place each year at the All England Lawn Tennis and Croquet Club. It is the oldest tennis tournament in the world and the only ‘Grand Slam’ event played on grass.

question_en: Which of the following is a British invention of the 20th century?
explanation_en: The Harrier jump jet , an aircraft capable of taking off vertically, is a British invention of the 20th century.

question_en: Churchill was the son of a politician and, before becoming a Conservative MP in 1900, was a soldier and journalist.
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: During the 17th century there were two main groups in Parliament, which were known as:

options.en: The Liberal Democrats and the Labour Party
The lefties and the right wing
The Conservative Party and the Liberal Democrats

explanation_en: During the 17th century there were two main groups in Parliament, known as the Whigs and the Tories .

question_en: Where is the ‘Eden Project’ located?
explanation_en: The Eden Project is located in Cornwall , in the south west of England.

question_en: When was the Women’s Social and Political Union (WSPU) established?
explanation_en: Emmeline Pankhurst was born in Manchester in 1858. She set up the women’s Franchise League in 1889, which fought to get the vote in local elections for married women. In 1903 she helped found the Women’s Social and Political Union (WSPU).

question_en: Which of the following is a traditional food from Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: Ulster fry – a fried meal with bacon, eggs, sausage, black pudding, tomatoes, mushrooms, soda bread and potato bread – is a traditional food from Northern Ireland.

question_en: Who is the author of the murder mystery play ‘The Mousetrap’?
explanation_en: The Mousetrap, a murder-mystery play by Dame Agatha Christie , has been running in the west end since 1952 and has had the longest initial run of any show in history.

question_en: Under which Act was the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland created?
explanation_en: In 1801, Ireland became unified with England, Scotland and Wales after the Act of Union of 1800. This created the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland.

question_en: When was the voting age reduced to 18 for men and women?
explanation_en: In 1969 , the voting age was reduced to 18 for men and women.

question_en: When is April Fool’s Day?
explanation_en: April Fool’s Day is on the 1st of April .

question_en: Where is the city of Bradford located?
explanation_en: Bradford is located in England .

question_en: Which flag comprises a red cross on a white ground?

options.en: The cross of St Andrew, patron saint of Scotland
The cross of St David, patron saint of Wales
The cross of St Patrick, patron saint of Ireland
The cross of St George, patron saint of England

explanation_en: The cross of St George, patron saint of England, is a red cross on a white ground.

question_en: When did the first farmers arrive in Britain?
explanation_en: The first farmers arrived in Britain 6,000 years ago .
